title: Check prime
description: Determine if a number is prime

examples.explanation: Step 1: n = 7, check if n < 2 → 7 ≥ 2, continue. Step 2: Start loop i from 2 to 6. i=2: 7 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. i=3: 7 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. i=4: 7 % 4 = 3 ≠ 0, continue. i=5: 7 % 5 = 2 ≠ 0, continue. i=6: 7 % 6 = 1 ≠ 0, loop ends. Step 3: No divisor found, return True.
Step 1: n = 9, check if n < 2 → 9 ≥ 2, continue. Step 2: Start loop i from 2 to 8. i=2: 9 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. i=3: 9 % 3 = 0, divisor found. Step 3: Early return False.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Check divisibility by all integers from 2 to n-1. It is straightforward but inefficient for large numbers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Prime Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking divisibility from 2 to n-1
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to checking if a number is prime relies on the fundamental definition: a prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The intuition is straightforward: if we can find any integer between 2 and n-1 that divides n evenly (with remainder 0), then n is composite (not prime). If no such divisor exists, n is prime. This works because any composite number must have at least one divisor in that range. The algorithm starts by handling edge cases: numbers less than 2 are not prime by definition. Then it iterates through all integers from 2 to n-1, checking if n modulo i equals 0. If any divisor is found, return false immediately. If the loop completes without finding a divisor, return true. Time complexity is O(n) because in the worst case (when n is prime), we perform n-2 iterations. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the divisibility check loop. Common mistakes include forgetting edge cases (n ≤ 1), inefficiently checking up to n instead of n-1, or not optimizing with early termination. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, n): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 7, check if n < 2 → 7 ≥ 2, continue. Step 2: Start loop i from 2 to 6. i=2: 7 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. i=3: 7 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. i=4: 7 % 4 = 3 ≠ 0, continue. i=5: 7 % 5 = 2 ≠ 0, continue. i=6: 7 % 6 = 1 ≠ 0, loop ends. Step 3: No divisor found, return True.
Step 1: n = 9, check if n < 2 → 9 ≥ 2, continue. Step 2: Start loop i from 2 to 8. i=2: 9 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. i=3: 9 % 3 = 0, divisor found. Step 3: Early return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Divisibility Check Loop
ladders.desc: Iterate through potential divisors from 2 to n-1
ladders.explanation: The divisibility check loop is the core of the brute force prime checking algorithm. The intuition is that to determine if n is prime, we need to test whether any integer in the range [2, n-1] divides n without remainder. This works because if n is composite, it must have at least one divisor in this range (specifically, the smallest divisor greater than 1). The algorithm involves initializing a loop variable i starting at 2, incrementing by 1 each iteration until i reaches n-1. In each iteration, compute n % i (remainder of n divided by i). If the remainder is 0, i is a divisor, so we can immediately conclude n is not prime and break out of the loop. If the loop completes without finding such an i, then no divisors exist, and n is prime. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) in the worst case when n is prime, as it performs n-2 iterations. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem (L0) by providing the mechanism to test divisibility. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., checking up to n instead of n-1), not using early termination when a divisor is found, or inefficient loop conditions. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, n): if n % i == 0: return False; return True (assuming edge cases handled elsewhere).

examples.trace: Step 1: i=2, compute 5 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, continue. Step 2: i=3, compute 5 % 3 = 2 ≠ 0, continue. Step 3: i=4, compute 5 % 4 = 1 ≠ 0, loop ends. Step 4: No divisor found, return True.
Step 1: i=2, compute 8 % 2 = 0, divisor found. Step 2: Early return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Testing Divisibility by a Single Number
ladders.desc: Check if one number divides another without remainder
ladders.explanation: The core logic of prime checking is testing divisibility: determining if an integer a divides another integer b evenly, meaning b % a == 0. The intuition is based on the definition of division: if a divides b, then b can be expressed as a × k for some integer k, leaving no remainder. In the context of prime checking, we test if any potential divisor i divides n. This works because if such an i exists (where 1 < i < n), then n is composite. The algorithm for a single test is simple: compute the remainder when n is divided by i using the modulo operator (%). If the remainder is 0, i is a divisor; otherwise, it is not. Time complexity for a single test is O(1), and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the divisibility check loop (L1) by being the operation performed in each iteration. Common mistakes include confusing division with integer division, not handling edge cases like division by zero, or misunderstanding the modulo operator. Real code pattern: if n % i == 0: return False else: continue.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 10 % 3. Step 2: 10 divided by 3 is 3 with remainder 1. Step 3: Since remainder 1 ≠ 0, 3 does not divide 10 evenly.
Step 1: Compute 10 % 5. Step 2: 10 divided by 5 is 2 with remainder 0. Step 3: Since remainder 0, 5 divides 10 evenly.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation, denoted as a % b, computes the remainder when a is divided by b. The intuition is that division of integers often does not result in a whole number; the remainder is what's left over after dividing as much as possible. For example, 7 ÷ 3 = 2 remainder 1, so 7 % 3 = 1. This works because for integers a and b (with b > 0), a can be expressed as a = b × q + r, where q is the quotient and r is the remainder with 0 ≤ r < b. The modulo operation returns r. In prime checking, we use n % i to test if i divides n: if the remainder is 0, i is a divisor. The algorithm involves performing integer division and extracting the remainder. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into testing divisibility (L2) by providing the means to check if remainder is zero. Common mistakes include misunderstanding negative numbers (in most languages, remainder takes sign of dividend), division by zero errors, or confusing modulo with integer division. Real code pattern: remainder = n % i.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 10 by 4. Step 2: 4 × 2 = 8, remainder 10 - 8 = 2. Step 3: So 10 % 4 = 2.
Step 1: Divide 15 by 5. Step 2: 5 × 3 = 15, remainder 15 - 15 = 0. Step 3: So 15 % 5 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division
ladders.desc: Divide two integers and get the quotient
ladders.explanation: Integer division is the operation of dividing one integer by another and discarding the remainder, resulting in an integer quotient. The intuition is that when dividing whole numbers, we often want how many times the divisor fits completely into the dividend. For example, 7 ÷ 3 = 2 because 3 fits into 7 two times (with 1 left over). This works because for integers a and b (b ≠ 0), the quotient q is the largest integer such that b × q ≤ a. In programming, this is often done using the // operator in languages like Python. In prime checking, integer division is implicitly used in the modulo operation to compute the remainder. The algorithm involves repeated subtraction or using built-in operators. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into the modulo operation (L3) as it's part of computing the remainder. Common mistakes include confusion with floating-point division, division by zero, or incorrect handling of negative numbers. Real code pattern: quotient = a // b.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 10 by 3. Step 2: 3 × 3 = 9 ≤ 10, 3 × 4 = 12 > 10. Step 3: So quotient is 3.
Step 1: Divide 15 by 5. Step 2: 5 × 3 = 15 ≤ 15. Step 3: So quotient is 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Looping Through a Range of Numbers
ladders.desc: Iterate sequentially from a start to an end value
ladders.explanation: Looping through a range of numbers is a fundamental programming concept where we execute a block of code repeatedly for each integer in a specified sequence. The intuition is that many problems require performing the same operation on multiple values, and loops provide a way to automate this without writing redundant code. For example, to check divisibility by all numbers from 2 to n-1, we use a loop to test each one. This works by initializing a counter variable at the start value, executing the loop body, incrementing the counter, and repeating until the counter passes the end value. In prime checking, this allows us to test each potential divisor efficiently. The algorithm typically uses a for loop: for i in range(start, end): # do something with i. Time complexity depends on the number of iterations; for a range of size m, it's O(m). Space complexity is O(1) if only a constant amount of extra memory is used. This feeds into the divisibility check loop (L1) by providing the structure to iterate through divisors. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using wrong bounds), infinite loops from incorrect increments, or modifying the loop variable incorrectly. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, n): # check n % i.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=1. Step 2: Execute body for i=1. Step 3: Increment i to 2, execute body. Step 4: Increment i to 3, execute body. Step 5: Increment i to 4, which is beyond end, loop stops.

Step 1: Initialize i=2. Step 2: Execute body for i=2. Step 3: Increment i to 3, execute body. Step 4: Increment i to 4, execute body. Step 5: Increment i to 5, execute body. Step 6: Increment i to 6, loop stops.

approaches.name: Optimized Division O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Check divisibility only up to the square root of n, since any factor larger than sqrt(n) must pair with a smaller one. This is the standard efficient method for prime checking.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Prime with Optimized Division
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of prime checking using sqrt(n) optimization
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to determine if a number n is prime using the optimized division method. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. The naive approach would check divisibility by all numbers from 2 to n-1, which is O(n). The key optimization is that we only need to check divisors up to sqrt(n). Why does this work? If n has a divisor d greater than sqrt(n), then the complementary divisor n/d must be less than sqrt(n) (since d * (n/d) = n, and if both were > sqrt(n), their product would exceed n). Therefore, if n has any divisors other than 1 and itself, at least one of them must be ≤ sqrt(n). The algorithm handles edge cases: numbers ≤ 1 are not prime, even numbers > 2 are not prime, and we can skip even divisors after checking 2. Step-by-step: 1) If n ≤ 1, return false. 2) If n == 2, return true. 3) If n % 2 == 0, return false. 4) For i from 3 to sqrt(n), step 2 (check only odd numbers): if n % i == 0, return false. 5) Return true. Time complexity: O(sqrt(n)) because the loop runs up to sqrt(n) iterations. Space complexity: O(1) as we use only constant extra space. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete prime checking logic that uses the sqrt optimization as its core. Common mistakes include: not handling n ≤ 1, checking up to n instead of sqrt(n), not optimizing for even numbers, and integer overflow when computing sqrt(n) (use i*i ≤ n instead of i ≤ sqrt(n) to avoid floating point issues). Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n <= 1: return False; if n == 2: return True; if n % 2 == 0: return False; i = 3; while i*i <= n: if n % i == 0: return False; i += 2; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=17 > 1, continue. Step 2: n≠2, continue. Step 3: 17%2=1 ≠0, continue. Step 4: i=3, 3*3=9 ≤17, 17%3=2 ≠0, i=5, 5*5=25 >17, loop ends. Step 5: return True.
Step 1: n=15 >1, continue. Step 2: n≠2, continue. Step 3: 15%2=1 ≠0, continue. Step 4: i=3, 3*3=9 ≤15, 15%3=0, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Divisibility Up to Sqrt(n)
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that checks divisibility from 3 to sqrt(n) for odd numbers
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the optimized prime checking algorithm. Given that we've already handled numbers ≤1, 2, and even numbers >2, this function checks if n is divisible by any odd number from 3 up to sqrt(n). The intuition is that after excluding even divisors, any remaining potential divisor must be odd. By checking only up to sqrt(n), we achieve O(sqrt(n)) time instead of O(n). Why check only odd numbers? Because if n is not divisible by 2 (already checked), it cannot be divisible by any even number (since even numbers are multiples of 2). The algorithm: Input n (assumed >2 and odd). Initialize i=3. While i*i ≤ n: if n % i == 0, return false (n is composite); i += 2. After loop, return true (n is prime). Time: O(sqrt(n)) as loop runs approximately sqrt(n)/2 iterations. Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the core divisibility check after edge cases are handled. Common mistakes: using i ≤ sqrt(n) with floating point sqrt (can cause precision errors), not incrementing by 2 (wasting time on even divisors), or missing the i*i ≤ n condition. Real code pattern: def check_odd_divisors(n): i=3; while i*i <= n: if n%i==0: return False; i+=2; return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sqrt(n) Optimization Principle
ladders.desc: Understand why checking divisors only up to sqrt(n) is sufficient
ladders.explanation: This is the central mathematical insight that makes prime checking efficient. For any composite number n, it can be expressed as n = a * b, where a and b are divisors greater than 1. The key observation is that if both a and b were greater than sqrt(n), then a * b > sqrt(n) * sqrt(n) = n, which contradicts n = a * b. Therefore, at least one of the divisors must be ≤ sqrt(n). This means that to determine if n is prime, we only need to check for divisors up to sqrt(n)—if none are found, n must be prime. Why? Because if n had a divisor d > sqrt(n), then n/d would be a divisor < sqrt(n), and we would have already found it. This reduces the number of checks from O(n) to O(sqrt(n)), a massive improvement for large n. For example, for n=1,000,000, we check up to 1,000 instead of 1,000,000. The algorithm: Given n, iterate i from 2 to sqrt(n): if n % i == 0, return composite. If loop completes, return prime. Time: O(sqrt(n)). Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 by justifying why the loop in the sub-routine only goes to sqrt(n). Common mistakes: misunderstanding the proof, checking up to n/2 instead (which is better than n but worse than sqrt(n)), or not considering the case when n is a perfect square. Real code pattern: def sqrt_optimization(n): for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=36, sqrt(36)=6. Check i=2: 36%2=0, divisor found, return False (composite). We don't need to check beyond 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Divisibility of a Number
ladders.desc: Determine if one number divides another evenly
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to prime checking: determining if a number a divides another number b without remainder. In the context of primes, we check if any number between 2 and sqrt(n) divides n. The operation uses the modulo operator (%), which returns the remainder of division. If b % a == 0, then a divides b evenly, meaning b is a multiple of a. For prime checking, if we find any a (where 1 < a < n) such that n % a == 0, then n is composite (not prime). The intuition is that divisibility implies the existence of factors. Algorithm: Given two integers a and b, return True if b % a == 0, else False. Time: O(1) for constant-time modulo operation. Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 as the basic operation used within the sqrt(n) loop to test each potential divisor. Common mistakes: confusing division (/) with modulo (%), checking a == 0 (division by zero error), or not handling negative numbers (in prime checking we deal with positives). Real code pattern: def divides(a, b): return b % a == 0.

examples.trace: a=3, b=12. Compute 12 % 3 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 12 evenly. Return True.
a=5, b=12. Compute 12 % 5 = 2. Since remainder is 2 (not 0), 5 does not divide 12 evenly. Return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Square Root or Equivalent
ladders.desc: Find the integer square root or use i*i ≤ n comparison
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is needed to implement the sqrt(n) optimization without floating-point issues. Since we need to check divisors up to sqrt(n), we must determine this bound. Directly computing sqrt(n) using math.sqrt(n) can introduce floating-point precision errors, especially for large integers. A safer, integer-only approach is to loop while i*i ≤ n. This avoids floating-point operations entirely and is exact. Why i*i ≤ n? Because if i is the largest integer ≤ sqrt(n), then i*i ≤ n, but (i+1)*(i+1) > n. So the condition i*i ≤ n ensures we check all i up to floor(sqrt(n)). For example, for n=10, sqrt(10)≈3.16, floor is 3, and we check i=2,3 (since 3*3=9 ≤10, 4*4=16 >10). Algorithm: Given n, initialize i=1; while i*i ≤ n: i+=1; after loop, the largest i checked is floor(sqrt(n)). Alternatively, in prime checking, we use this directly in the loop condition. Time: O(sqrt(n)) if computing up to sqrt(n), but in context it's part of the larger loop. Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the bound for the divisibility checks. Common mistakes: using i ≤ sqrt(n) with floating point, not handling large n where i*i might overflow (use 64-bit integers), or misunderstanding the inequality. Real code pattern: In loop: while i*i <= n: ...

examples.trace: n=16. i=1: 1*1=1 ≤16, continue; i=2: 4≤16; i=3: 9≤16; i=4: 16≤16; i=5: 25>16, stop. Largest i with i*i≤16 is 4, which is sqrt(16).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Prime Number?
ladders.desc: Define prime numbers and basic properties
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a prime number is. A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are prime because they can only be divided evenly by 1 and themselves. Numbers greater than 1 that are not prime are called composite numbers; they have more than two divisors (e.g., 4 has divisors 1,2,4). The number 1 is neither prime nor composite by definition. Why is this important? It sets the goal for the algorithm: given n, we want to determine if it fits this definition. The intuition for checking is to test if any number between 2 and n-1 divides n—if yes, n is composite; if no, n is prime. This naive check is the starting point that we optimize later. Basic algorithm: if n ≤ 1, return false; for i from 2 to n-1: if n % i == 0, return false; return true. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). This feeds into L4 by establishing why we need to check divisibility. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, forgetting to check n=2, or not understanding the 'greater than 1' condition. Real code pattern: def is_prime_naive(n): if n<=1: return False; for i in range(2,n): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: n=8 >1. Check i=2: 8%2=0, divisor found, so 8 is composite (not prime).
